Although the Verification Report of Kikonda project GS 2990 (Page 21) mentions a difference of 18,747 tCO2e in soil organic carbon stock, this value does not provide a percentage change. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Monitoring  of  SDG13:  Data  and  parameters  monitored  under  this  SDG  are as follows:  Diameter  at  Breast  height  (DBH):  Forest  inventory  data  sheets  are  •  reviewed  and  verified  for  the  monitoring  of  the  data  for  its  compliance  with registered Monitoring Plan. Height: The EPIC audit team verified the monitoring of the data through  •  data  sheets  and  visit  to  sample  biomass  plots. T   A   1   B     A The Kikonda project (GS 2990) have been through a Change of Ownership as well aside from the  design change. The Change of Ownership also required being compliant with GS criteria as per the  SustainCERT  formal  requirement.  NFB  as  the  new  owners  were  able  to  adhere  to  these  requirements (see file below) based on the confirmation from SustainCERT.   he design change does not affect the applicability of the methodology, baseline and additionality  through the following:  pplicability of the methodology: The project is compliant with the applicability conditions of the  methodology  since  the  same  applicability  and  eligibility  criteria  (from  the  LUF  Activity  Requirements  and  GS  Principles  and  Requirements)  were  already  validated  and  certified  before.  The  project  has  implemented  changes  on  the  rotation  period  and  management  activities,  but  all  other aspects were retained. Example of aspects included in the applicability criteria are:  .  Eligible  project  areas  where  VERs  can  be  claimed  (must  be  deforested  in  the  last  10  years  before  project  start  date).  This  remained  constant  since  all  reforestation  activities  (since  the  beginning of the project until this monitoring period) were carried out inside the original 7,321  hectares of eligible areas that were already validated and certified.   2.  Secured  title  and  ownership  of  the  project  area.  While  the  project  owners  changed,  the  ownership  and  accompanying  rights  to  the  project  area  and  corresponding  VERs  were  demonstrated to be not affected due to the compliance of the new owners to the GS Change of  Ownership Process (as confirmed by both GS and SustainCERT, see files below) that also has  its  own  due  diligence  and  requirements  specific  for  the  land  ownership  aspect.  Additionally,  during the on-site visit  the project  owners were  able to  provide all the legal  evidence proving  that  they  have  the  rights  over  the  project  areas  through  various  legal  certificates  from  the  National Forest Authority.   3.  As an A/R project, the LUF Activity requirements allows the following:  a.  Can include planting trees  b.  Can include single-species plantations  c.  Can apply all silvicultural systems. VVB assessment   Date: 17/11/2023: The  project  developer  resubmitted  the  GS  spatial  data  and  the  EPIC  audit  team  reviewed  the  same  to  confirm that the data extracted for the area under conservation from the spatial files and in the MR are  similar and is correct. The total project area is 12, 186 ha and there is a difference of 5.2 ha between the  data  from  the  shape  files  and  the  data  in  the  tree  license  certificate  from  NFA.  The  project  developer  justified the reasons for the same that is the 5.7 hectares is an integral part of the natural belt alongside the  Kafu River and is not surveyed properly to account that area. The same issue was discussed with the NFA  officials  from  the  EPIC  audit  team  to  verify  the  compliance  of  the  project  area  with  the  data  extracted  from GIS analysis. The confirmation mail from the NFA is attached as a reference in the appendix where  the NFA confirmed about the project area. The responses from  the project developer for the finding is  appropriate and the  CAR is closed.

T   A   1   B     A The Kikonda project (GS 2990) have been through a Change of Ownership as well aside from the  design change. The Change of Ownership also required being compliant with GS criteria as per the  SustainCERT  formal  requirement.  NFB  as  the  new  owners  were  able  to  adhere  to  these  requirements (see file below) based on the confirmation from SustainCERT.   he design change does not affect the applicability of the methodology, baseline and additionality  through the following:  pplicability of the methodology: The project is compliant with the applicability conditions of the  methodology  since  the  same  applicability  and  eligibility  criteria  (from  the  LUF  Activity  Requirements  and  GS  Principles  and  Requirements)  were  already  validated  and  certified  before.  The  project  has  implemented  changes  on  the  rotation  period  and  management  activities,  but  all  other aspects were retained. Example of aspects included in the applicability criteria are:  .  Eligible  project  areas  where  VERs  can  be  claimed  (must  be  deforested  in  the  last  10  years  before  project  start  date).  This  remained  constant  since  all  reforestation  activities  (since  the  beginning of the project until this monitoring period) were carried out inside the original 7,321  hectares of eligible areas that were already validated and certified.   2.  Secured  title  and  ownership  of  the  project  area.  While  the  project  owners  changed,  the  ownership  and  accompanying  rights  to  the  project  area  and  corresponding  VERs  were  demonstrated to be not affected due to the compliance of the new owners to the GS Change of  Ownership Process (as confirmed by both GS and SustainCERT, see files below) that also has  its  own  due  diligence  and  requirements  specific  for  the  land  ownership  aspect.  Additionally,  during the on-site visit  the project  owners were  able to  provide all the legal  evidence proving  that  they  have  the  rights  over  the  project  areas  through  various  legal  certificates  from  the  National Forest Authority.   3.  As an A/R project, the LUF Activity requirements allows the following:  a.  Can include planting trees  b.  Can include single-species plantations  c.  Can apply all silvicultural systems. For example:  i.  Conservation forests (no use of timber)  ii.  Forests with selective harvesting  iii.  Rotation forestry  The  project  continued  to  be  compliant  with  the  above  requirements  since  the  project  start  date (2002) until this monitoring period.   aseline:  The  project  implemented  reforestation  activities  inside  the  original  7,321  hectares  of  eligible areas that were already validated and certified. The eligible areas did not change since the  project started and the basis for their eligibility holds true until this monitoring period. The project  has implemented changes on the rotation period and management  activities, but  the eligible areas  and the basis of their eligibility did not change since the project started.   dditionality: The previous project owners included investment analysis as part of the additionality  component, in this monitoring period with the new project owners, this aspect was considered and  provided in the revised PDD demonstrating that the project is still additional. The financial analysis  also serves as supporting documentation for this component.: Documentation provided by project participant

3. Good health and well being.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, 'Area under reduced/avoided open burning of biomass, crop residue'. Although the project developer deducted 10% of the baseline for biomass burning (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 26), and the EPIC audit team noticed the burning of biomass in the field during an onsite visit for land preparation (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 33), no specific area is mentioned for the implementation of alternative technologies or measures to avoid open biomass/crop residue burning. The actual areas set aside for conservation amounted to 1,958 hectares, which translates to 16% of the project area (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 50), but this does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the score is 0.

On the ground, the final 
decision is taken whether an area may be cleared or whether it is identified as 
Conservation Area.
The land to be cleared is then assigned to contractors who are in charge of the actual 
field work.
In most cases, contractors apply manual or semi-motorized clearance.
That 
means that shrubs, bushes and grass are cut manually using axes and pangas.
In case 
single trees need to be felled, chainsaws are used.
The biomass is then heaped and

Good health and well being.summary: The evidence from the Verification Report of Kikonda project (GS 2990) on pages 9 and 12 provides some qualitative information about the project's activities, but it does not directly measure the number of farmers who have adopted alternative measures to prevent open burning of biomass and crop residue. The report mentions training on energy-saving cook stoves, creation of awareness, and design changes to allow for crop growth between tree plantings, but these are not explicitly linked to a specific number of farmers. The CO2Fixation tool V0.9 is used for afforestation and reforestation activities, but this does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative, and the score is 1.

4. Quality education.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable, 'Number of employees provided skill development training'. Although the total number of employees hired under a permanent or temporal contract agreement is provided as 5,855, the number of employees who received skill development training is not specified. The types of training services provided include occupational safety, training on first aid and safety, workers' rights, and training on energy-saving cook stoves, but no quantitative data is provided to support the indicator. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative, and the score is 1.

4. Quality education.indicator: Number of employees provided skill development training

4. Quality education.summary: The evidence from the Kikonda Project-Annual-Report-GS+2990_2023.pdf, Page 5, mentions job creation data with 21 permanent male employees and 5 permanent female employees, and 279 temporary male contractual employees and 45 temporary female contractual employees. However, this data does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Number of training hours provided for employees'. The evidence from the Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page 23, mentions equal opportunities for skills development, but this is a qualitative statement and does not provide explicit quantitative data. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

5. Gender equality.summary: The evidence provided for the SDG indicator 5.5.2 Proportion of women in managerial positions is weak and indirect. The total number of managerial staff for the project from 2013 to 2020 is 152, but the total number of female managerial staff for the same period is 48. Although the proportion of females in managerial positions compared to the total number of persons employed in managerial positions is mentioned, it is based on incomplete data. The proportion is calculated as 5/26, but the total number of managerial staff in 2023 is not directly provided. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, and the score is 0.

VVB assessment   Date: 17/11/2023: The  project  developer  resubmitted  the  GS  spatial  data  and  the  EPIC  audit  team  reviewed  the  same  to  confirm that the data extracted for the area under conservation from the spatial files and in the MR are  similar and is correct. The total project area is 12, 186 ha and there is a difference of 5.2 ha between the  data  from  the  shape  files  and  the  data  in  the  tree  license  certificate  from  NFA.  The  project  developer  justified the reasons for the same that is the 5.7 hectares is an integral part of the natural belt alongside the  Kafu River and is not surveyed properly to account that area. The same issue was discussed with the NFA  officials  from  the  EPIC  audit  team  to  verify  the  compliance  of  the  project  area  with  the  data  extracted  from GIS analysis. The confirmation mail from the NFA is attached as a reference in the appendix where  the NFA confirmed about the project area. The responses from  the project developer for the finding is  appropriate and the  CAR is closed.

Gender equality.summary: No explicit quantitative data is found in the provided context to directly measure the average time saving associated with cooking time and fuel collection due to the adoption of project technology. However, it is mentioned that households received training on energy-saving cook stoves, which may have reduced the time spent on cooking and fuel collection (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 9). Some community members mentioned benefits from the project, including the collection of firewood (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 9), but no specific information is provided on the average time saving. The evidence is qualitative only, and therefore, the score is 1.

T Gold  Standard  started  implementing  the  SDG  requirement  when  the  GS4GG  document  was  launched in 2017. Prior to this, the SDG requirements was not required for GS projects. Although,  the Kikonda  Forest  Reserve Project  (implemented since 2002) have included socio-economic  and  environmental parameters in the previous validated and certified project design documents (PDD).  In these previously validated and certified PDDs, the parameters on reduction of emissions through  reforestation  (related  to  SDG  13),  employment  generation  (related  to  SDG  8)  and  areas  where  reforestation occurred (related to SDG 15) were mentioned and indicated that these parameters are  what  the  project  intends  to  contribute  and  monitor  on  the  socio-economic  and  environmental  aspects (which would later be converted to the SDG contributions). There was no explicit mention  of the sustainable consumption and production parameter (and its future monitoring) in the previous  validated and certified PDDs.   his  monitoring  period  (2016-2021)  certification  is  the  first  instance  (after  the  SDG  certification  was required by GS) that the project included an explicit SDG information and monitored data that  were  mentioned  in  the  previous  PDDs.  Hence,  we  consider  that  there  was  not  a  change  of  SDG  (from  12  to  8),  but  a  mere  clarification  that  SDGs  13,  8  and  15  were  the  initial/original  SDG  contributions  since  the  beginning  of  the  project  and  continues  to  be  the  identified  SDGs  in  this  monitoring period certification.: D ocumentation provided by project participant

VVB assessment   Date: 17/11/2023: The  project  developer  resubmitted  the  GS  spatial  data  and  the  EPIC  audit  team  reviewed  the  same  to  confirm that the data extracted for the area under conservation from the spatial files and in the MR are  similar and is correct. The total project area is 12, 186 ha and there is a difference of 5.2 ha between the  data  from  the  shape  files  and  the  data  in  the  tree  license  certificate  from  NFA.  The  project  developer  justified the reasons for the same that is the 5.7 hectares is an integral part of the natural belt alongside the  Kafu River and is not surveyed properly to account that area. The same issue was discussed with the NFA  officials  from  the  EPIC  audit  team  to  verify  the  compliance  of  the  project  area  with  the  data  extracted  from GIS analysis. The confirmation mail from the NFA is attached as a reference in the appendix where  the NFA confirmed about the project area. The responses from  the project developer for the finding is  appropriate and the  CAR is closed.

secondary forest.
Therefore, the only forest resource with economical potential are the 
trees planted.

Affordable and clean energy.summary: The provided context does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures the number of unique households provided access to clean fuels and technologies. Although the context mentions that a total of 250ha have been planted in 2023 and 520.09 ha have been reforested, these numbers do not measure the indicator variable directly. The context also mentions that the project allows community people to collect firewood from the project area, once in a week, which is also a major livelihood support for the community people, but this information is qualitative only. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence provided in the Monitoring Report (Document: 11.06.2024_GS+2990_T-PerfCert_V1.1-Monitoring-Report.pdf, Page: 4) states that the total number of jobs generated as a result of the project is 1622. However, this is an incomplete measurement as it does not account for the accumulated value of jobs generated until the end of the current monitoring period (2016-2021) as mentioned in the Verification Report of Kikonda project (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 29). The Monitoring Report also provides a breakdown of the number of jobs generated from 2017 to 2021, which is an average of 520 jobs per year. Furthermore, the Verification Report of Kikonda project (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 20) provides detailed data on the permanent and temporal workers employed from the project, which includes 258 permanent workers and 240 contractors in 2022. However, the total number of jobs generated as a result of the project is explicitly stated as 2622 in the Intermediate Evidence-Based Answers, which is a more comprehensive measurement. Therefore, the evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total number of employees earning above local minimum wage' as the local minimum wage is not explicitly stated. The Verification Report of Kikonda project mentions that the project respects workers' rights, but it does not provide information on the local minimum wage. The percentage of employees earning above the local minimum wage is mentioned as 5.4% without carbon consideration and 12.1% with carbon income during validation of the project, but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. Therefore, the score is 0.

8. Decent work and economic growth.target: 8.5 By 2030, achieve full and productive employment...
8. Decent work and economic growth.indicator: Total number of employees earning above local minimum wage

8. Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from the Verification Report of Kikonda project (GS 2990.pdf, Page 23) mentions that the project is in compliance with the principles and standards embodied in the International Labour Organization (ILO). However, it does not provide explicit information regarding the payment of a living wage to workers. The project management encourages workers and staff to join for work on their choice, but this does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

What is the extent to which employment o.content: : Findings | Means of verification  The  project  is  contributing  to  three  SDGs  i.e.,  SDG8,  SDG13  and  SDG  15.  The EPIC audit team reviewed the MR and conducted an onsite visit to verify  the contributions of the project towards sustainable development. The project  provided  employment  opportunities  for  the  community  people  and  helps  in  the  improvement  of  their  livelihood.  The  project allows  community  people to  collect  the  firewood  from  the  project  area,  once  in  a  week  which  is  also  a  major livelihood support for the community people. The project also mitigates  climate change by reducing GHG emissions.: No finding raised regarding this issue

The report also mentions community people collecting firewood (Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 32), but it does not provide any data on the number of person-hours created. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

The mention of employment opportunities is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The provided context does not contain explicit quantitative data to directly measure the average hourly earnings of employees by sex, age, occupation, and persons with disabilities. However, it mentions the creation of jobs, including 279 male contractual jobs and 45 female temporary jobs in 2023 (Document: Kikonda+Project-Annual-Report-GS+2990_2023.pdf, Page: 5). The total number of female permanent jobs created from 2013 to 2022 is also mentioned, but it does not provide information on earnings. The context provides information on the average number of employees hired during the monitoring period, which is 5,855 (Document: 11.06.2024_GS+2990_T-PerfCert_V1.1-Monitoring-Report.pdf, Page: 33). Since the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, the score is 0.

Climate action.summary: The Verification Report of Kikonda project (GS 2990, Page 23) states that the project is not increasing GHG emissions over the Baseline Scenario, which is a qualitative indication that climate change measures are integrated into national policies and practices. However, this does not directly measure the indicator variable. The report also mentions that the project mitigates climate change by reducing GHG emissions (GS 2990, Page 32), but this is a qualitative statement and does not provide explicit quantitative data. The EPIC audit team's conclusion that the project is contributing to sustainable development and climate benefits (GS 2990, Page 32) is also qualitative. The only quantitative data mentioned is that the project allows community people to collect firewood from the project area, once a week, but this does not measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence for the indicator 'Forest areas managed sustainably for forest products including sustainable produced fuelwood' is partially available. The total area of forest land under sustainable management practices is 7,321 hectares, which is the eligible area for planting (Verification Report of the Kikonda project, Page 33). However, the percentage of forest areas certified under recognized sustainable forest management standards is unknown due to the lack of supporting evidence. The types of forest management practices being implemented include rotation forestry, and the key factors influencing the adoption of sustainable forest management practices among forest landowners/operators include community involvement and support. The average annual rate of increase in the area of forest land under sustainable management practices is unknown due to the lack of supporting evidence. Therefore, the score is 2.

T Gold  Standard  started  implementing  the  SDG  requirement  when  the  GS4GG  document  was  launched in 2017. Prior to this, the SDG requirements was not required for GS projects. Although,  the Kikonda  Forest  Reserve Project  (implemented since 2002) have included socio-economic  and  environmental parameters in the previous validated and certified project design documents (PDD).  In these previously validated and certified PDDs, the parameters on reduction of emissions through  reforestation  (related  to  SDG  13),  employment  generation  (related  to  SDG  8)  and  areas  where  reforestation occurred (related to SDG 15) were mentioned and indicated that these parameters are  what  the  project  intends  to  contribute  and  monitor  on  the  socio-economic  and  environmental  aspects (which would later be converted to the SDG contributions). There was no explicit mention  of the sustainable consumption and production parameter (and its future monitoring) in the previous  validated and certified PDDs.   his  monitoring  period  (2016-2021)  certification  is  the  first  instance  (after  the  SDG  certification  was required by GS) that the project included an explicit SDG information and monitored data that  were  mentioned  in  the  previous  PDDs.  Hence,  we  consider  that  there  was  not  a  change  of  SDG  (from  12  to  8),  but  a  mere  clarification  that  SDGs  13,  8  and  15  were  the  initial/original  SDG  contributions  since  the  beginning  of  the  project  and  continues  to  be  the  identified  SDGs  in  this  monitoring period certification.: S     V See  file  Gold  Standard  Project  Design  Document  (PDD  -THE  KIKONDA  FOREST  RESERVE- 18Apr2013.pdf)  https://platform.sustain-cert.com/public-luf-project/2784  ee file GS 2990_PDD 2009  (Folder 17_Previous PDDs GS 2990)

Eligible  project  areas  where  VERs  can  be  claimed  (must  be  deforested  in  the  last  10  years  before  project  start  date).  This  remained  constant  since  all  reforestation  activities  (since  the  beginning of the project until this monitoring period) were carried out inside the original 7,321  hectares of eligible areas that were already validated and certified.   2.  Secured  title  and  ownership  of  the  project  area.  While  the  project  owners  changed,  the  ownership  and  accompanying  rights  to  the  project  area  and  corresponding  VERs  were  demonstrated to be not affected due to the compliance of the new owners to the GS Change of  Ownership Process (as confirmed by both GS and SustainCERT, see files below) that also has  its  own  due  diligence  and  requirements  specific  for  the  land  ownership  aspect.  Additionally,  during the on-site visit  the project  owners were  able to  provide all the legal  evidence proving  that  they  have  the  rights  over  the  project  areas  through  various  legal  certificates  from  the  National Forest Authority.   3.  As an A/R project, the LUF Activity requirements allows the following:  a.  Can include planting trees  b.  Can include single-species plantations  c.  Can apply all silvicultural systems. For example:  i.  Conservation forests (no use of timber)  ii.  Forests with selective harvesting  iii. 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the total above and below ground biomass stock in forests. Although the above ground biomass (AGB) for Eucalyptus and Pine species is estimated to be 3.4 and 1.3, respectively, and the below ground biomass (BGB) is estimated to be 0.68 and 0.299, respectively, these estimates are per hectare and do not account for the total area of forests. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator. The provided data is qualitative and does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures or clearly supports the indicator.

cattle keepers.

Date: 20/08/2023  Project Design Document: The approach of taking the height of five trees (3 trees with representative height and 2 tallest trees) is  conservative  as  these  are  based  on  published  guidelines  for  plantations  specific  for  Uganda.  The  published  guideline  (see  SPGS_Tree  Planting  Guidelines  for  Uganda)  provides  information  that  for  a  uniform crop/plantation of 10 hectares, 10 trees (i.e., 1 tree per hectare) will suffice to estimate the stand  height. Further, the guideline recommends measuring  the total  height of  the two trees of largest  dbh in  each plot (that gives the Top or Dominant Height). Hence, the approach used by the project for the tree  height  measurement  is  aligned  with  credible  and  scientific  publication  that  considers  uncertainty  and  conservativeness.

:  | : 4. | : Change  in  the  management  regime | limited and NFB is continuing the project  and is the new owner of the project.  •  Since 06th November 2020, the NFB has  been  holding  tree  farming  license  for  period of 31 years.  •  The  EPIC  audit  team  conducted  onsite  visit,  interviewed  with  the  project  personnel, and reviewed the tree license  dated  06th  Nov  2020  from  NFA  and  harvest permit from NFA to confirm that  the  NFB  is  the  present  owner  of  the  project and acquired the carbon rights of  the project.  •  The  cover  letter  of  the  revised  PDD,  verification  report  and  design  change   validation report is updated.: •  The  rotation  period  for  the  Pine  and  Eucalyptus  in  the  registered  PDD  is  18  years and 16 years respectively.   •  The  project  requested  to  reduce  the  rotation  period  of  Pine  from  18  years to  14  years  and  for  Eucalyptus  from  16  to  10 years.  •  The  EPIC  audit  team  interviewed  the  project  personnel  to  validate  the  reasons  behind reducing  rotation  period  and  its  impact  on  the  quantification  of  GHG emissions.  •  The EPIC audit team confirmed that the  end  use  of  the  harvested  wood  is  different for NFB than from the previous  owner.  •  NFB  produces  the  Medium  Density  Board  and  to  manufacture  the  MD  board,  the  wood  selected  is  chipped  using the chipper.  •  The  trees  with  higher  GBH  are  not  suitable  to  feed  into  the  chipper  machines. Hence the NFB requested for  reduction  in  the  rotation  period  of  both  the species.  •  The  EPIC  audit  team  confirmed  that  from  next  monitoring  period,  the  management regime is different and the  impacted  MUs  are  035  to  044.  Hence  the  EPIC  audit  team  is  of  the  opinion  that  the  Project  Developer  updated  Alder Model to combine the old and new  management applied to those MUs. 15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided in the Intermediate Answers and Context satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. The total land area of the project that has been designated as protected is 1,958 hectares, which translates to 16% of the project area. This figure is based on the actual areas set aside for conservation as the planting progressed, as confirmed through the review of spatial files and the Monitoring Report by the EPIC audit team (Document: Verification+Report+of+Kikonda+project_GS+2990.pdf, Page: 51). The data unit used to measure the protected land area is hectares (ha), which matches the indicator's data unit. The percentage of the project area that is protected land area is 16%, which meets the indicator threshold of >=10%. Therefore, the score is 2.

Date: 20/08/2023  Project Design Document: The approach of taking the height of five trees (3 trees with representative height and 2 tallest trees) is  conservative  as  these  are  based  on  published  guidelines  for  plantations  specific  for  Uganda.  The  published  guideline  (see  SPGS_Tree  Planting  Guidelines  for  Uganda)  provides  information  that  for  a  uniform crop/plantation of 10 hectares, 10 trees (i.e., 1 tree per hectare) will suffice to estimate the stand  height. Further, the guideline recommends measuring  the total  height of  the two trees of largest  dbh in  each plot (that gives the Top or Dominant Height). Hence, the approach used by the project for the tree  height  measurement  is  aligned  with  credible  and  scientific  publication  that  considers  uncertainty  and  conservativeness.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the Verification Report of Kikonda project (GS 2990) mentions that 16% of the project area (1,958 hectares) is under conservation, which is about 60% more than the area necessary for certification (Page 50). However, this information does not directly measure the number of protected threatened species in the project area. The EPIC audit team verified that the project area is under patrolling for conservation and that the plots harvested are reforested (Page 22), but this does not provide specific information about the protection of threatened species. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species diversity' as it only mentions the planting of Pine and Eucalyptus species, but does not provide information on the number of tree species or their diversity. Although the context mentions the initial planting spacing of 1,111 trees per hectare, this information does not provide any insight into tree species diversity. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the measurement requirement.

Climate Security and Sustainable Development 
The tropical high forest and the natural savannah forest that historically had covered 
the area had already disappeared completely before commencement of tree farming 
license.
Uncontrolled logging and encroachment continue to deplete the bushland and 
secondary forest.
Therefore, the only forest resource with economical potential are the 
trees planted.

However, it does not provide specific information on the impact of invasive species on native tree species. The evidence is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable, which refers to trends in species distribution. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement.

The Change of Ownership also required being compliant with GS criteria as per the  SustainCERT  formal  requirement.  NFB  as  the  new  owners  were  able  to  adhere  to  these  requirements (see file below) based on the confirmation from SustainCERT.   he design change does not affect the applicability of the methodology, baseline and additionality  through the following:  pplicability of the methodology: The project is compliant with the applicability conditions of the  methodology  since  the  same  applicability  and  eligibility  criteria  (from  the  LUF  Activity  Requirements  and  GS  Principles  and  Requirements)  were  already  validated  and  certified  before.  The  project  has  implemented  changes  on  the  rotation  period  and  management  activities,  but  all  other aspects were retained. Example of aspects included in the applicability criteria are:  .  Eligible  project  areas  where  VERs  can  be  claimed  (must  be  deforested  in  the  last  10  years  before  project  start  date).  This  remained  constant  since  all  reforestation  activities  (since  the  beginning of the project until this monitoring period) were carried out inside the original 7,321  hectares of eligible areas that were already validated and certified.   2.  Secured  title  and  ownership  of  the  project  area.  While  the  project  owners  changed,  the  ownership  and  accompanying  rights  to  the  project  area  and  corresponding  VERs  were  demonstrated to be not affected due to the compliance of the new owners to the GS Change of  Ownership Process (as confirmed by both GS and SustainCERT, see files below) that also has  its  own  due  diligence  and  requirements  specific  for  the  land  ownership  aspect.
